title: WFP Launches HungerMap Live — AI-Powered Food Security Intelligence Platform for 50+ Countries
detail: The World Food Programme launched HungerMap Live on April 16, a modernized next-generation digital intelligence platform that integrates food security data with AI-assisted predictive forecasting across more than 50 countries and 16 identified Hunger Hotspots. The platform consolidates insights from 300+ analysts and humanitarian partners, combining satellite imagery, market price data, conflict indicators, and weather forecasts to generate early warnings of emerging food crises. A key upgrade from the prior HungerMap is the AI-assisted anticipatory action capability: the system now identifies deteriorating food security trajectories weeks before they reach crisis level, enabling WFP and partners to pre-position resources and activate early interventions. Independent studies cited by WFP show that anticipatory humanitarian action yields a 7-to-1 return on investment compared to reactive emergency response — meaning earlier AI-triggered alerts directly translate to greater lives saved per dollar spent. The platform is freely accessible to all humanitarian organizations and national governments.

media.caption: WFP HungerMap Live platform provides AI-assisted food security forecasting across 50+ countries

title: WHO Eastern Mediterranean Launches AI Community of Practice for Disaster and Emergency Response
detail: The World Health Organization's Regional Office for the Eastern Mediterranean (EMRO) established a new AI Community of Practice on April 16, focused on strengthening capacity for artificial intelligence applications in disaster and emergency response surveillance. The collaborative platform, hosted via WHO Collaboratory, brings together national health authorities, academic researchers, and humanitarian partners from 21 EMRO member states — spanning North Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia — to share knowledge, training modules, and implementation guidance on responsible AI deployment during health emergencies and disasters. The initiative provides structured peer learning on use cases including AI-powered epidemiological surveillance during acute crises, satellite-based damage assessment, and AI-assisted coordination of medical response. For a region that faces frequent conflicts, earthquakes, floods, and disease outbreaks, strengthening national AI emergency response capacity addresses critical gaps in humanitarian preparedness — particularly relevant given complex emergencies in Yemen, Gaza, Sudan, and Syria affecting tens of millions of people.

media.caption: WHO EMRO launches AI Community of Practice for disaster response across 21 member states
